retrieved_docs.title: Child Development -- Function -- Landau Reflex
retrieved_docs.content: The Landau Reflex appears in the first 3 months. It is elicited by holding the baby in a supine position in mid-air. The response is an extension of the legs and head as if looking up. It disappears by 24 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Auditory Steady-State Response -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: ASSR results in higher hearing thresholds at low frequencies ≤0.5 kHz compared to high frequencies; this is attributed to various factors, including higher response amplitudes at higher frequencies, partially due to a lower EEG noise floor at these frequencies. [33] [35] [10] Misplaced earphones can also attenuate lower-frequency stimuli, and maturational changes can affect the transmission of low-frequency energy in infants' middle ears. Stimulation at lower frequencies affects a broader portion of the basilar membrane due to longer travel time for waves to reach the apical region of the cochlea, potentially leading to delayed jitter between receptors and stimulations. Additionally, there may be immature neural synchronization in low-frequency responses. [35] [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Auditory Steady-State Response -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: ASSR facilitates hearing aid fittings, especially in very young children whose hearing rehabilitation should be initiated as early as possible for reasons of speech development. In the past, the fitting of amplification in young children was often delayed. Free-field behavioral audiometry is unreliable in hearing aid fitting because it does not provide independent hearing thresholds for each ear. [8] [9] The use of ABR for hearing assessment in young children can also pose challenges due to the need for multiple appointments. ABR examination often lasts upwards of 60 minutes, longer than many children's natural sleep duration; patients often wake up before the test is finished. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Brainstem Auditory Evoked Response Test -- Issues of Concern -- Basic Concepts
retrieved_docs.content: Latency: The time it takes for each wave to occur after the presentation of the auditory stimulus. Latency values provide insight into the conduction speed along the auditory pathway. For example, longer latencies may indicate abnormalities in neural conduction or delays in transmitting auditory information, such as in conductive hearing loss. The latency of wave V is generally used as a marker for the robustness of the auditory response and should be approximately 7 milliseconds (ms) to 9 ms. Shifts in latency and amplitude are utilized to determine if a legitimate response is present. A louder intensity stimulus should correlate with a larger amplitude and shorter latency, while a quieter intensity stimulus should correspond to a smaller amplitude and longer latency. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Auditory Brainstem Response -- Technique or Treatment -- Interpretation
retrieved_docs.content: The primary measurements from an ABR are the absolute wave latencies, amplitudes, and interwave intervals between waves I to III, III to V, and I to V. The final ABR result is the averaged responses to several thousand repetitions of a stimulus. [20] Wave latency is defined by the time between the initial auditory stimulus and the peak of a wave. [18] Latencies can be affected by age and intensity of stimulation. Latencies are longer in newborns and shorten with age. As stimulus intensity increases, the latency also shortens. Decreased stimulus intensity is associated with longer absolute wave latencies, decreased wave peak amplitudes, and loss of waves. The height between the wave peak and the succeeding trough defines me, III, and V. Wave amplitude. The stimulus intensity and amount of synchronized neural activity determine the amplitude. The more fibers that are simultaneously activated, the larger the peak amplitude. [18] The ABR may be utilized to estimate hearing levels and can determine the type and degree of hearing loss if present. The threshold of ABR is the lowest stimulus intensity at which reproducible ABR waveforms can be detected. It provides a good estimation of the hearing sensitivity threshold. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Brainstem Auditory Evoked Response Test -- Issues of Concern -- Basic Concepts
retrieved_docs.content: Interpeak Intervals (IPIs): The time period between successive waves. They represent the time taken for neural signals to travel between specific auditory structures. They are calculated by subtracting the latency of one peak from the latency of the preceding peak. These IPIs are crucial for understanding the timing and coordination of neural activity along the auditory pathway. The approximate IPI for waves I-II are 1.5 ms to 2.5 ms, waves III-V are 1.5 ms to 2.5 ms, and waves I-V are 3.0 ms to 4.0 ms. It should be noted that IPIs can vary depending on stimuli and subject-specific factors. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Auditory Steady-State Response -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Hearing thresholds provided from prior tests, such as non–frequency-specific ABR, can provide the starting intensity or loudness points for the ASSR test. The remainder of the testing procedure is similar to conventional audiometry, focused on finding the hearing threshold at each tested frequency. [2] [26] For patients undergoing testing during natural sleep, larger steps of 20 dB up and down in intensity may be necessary to expedite the examination and prevent patients from awakening before the test is completed.

retrieved_docs.title: Brainstem Auditory Evoked Response Test -- Issues of Concern -- Waveforms
retrieved_docs.content: Wave IV: Following Wave III, Wave IV is a negative deflection representing the activation of the LL, a pathway involved in relaying auditory information from the brainstem to higher auditory centers. The summation of postsynaptic potentials in the LL neurons generates wave IV. It typically appears around 4 to 6 milliseconds after the stimulus onset. Wave IV is known for its robust and reliable waveform, showing consistent morphology and amplitude across recordings. However, it can be affected by factors such as the stimulus parameters, electrode placement, and the individual's state of consciousness. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Auditory Steady-State Response -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: ASSR was first described by Galambos et al in 1981, highlighting a prominent neural response to 40-Hz tonal stimuli in adults with normal hearing. Initially termed the 40-Hz event-related potential, or steady-state evoked potential, Galambos et al observed its distinctive and consistent presence near the behavioral hearing threshold, suggesting its potential as a predictor of objective hearing threshold. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Auditory Steady-State Response -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: ASSR testing offers the advantage of simultaneous multifrequency binaural testing, reducing testing time compared to that required for ABR. [2] This advantage is particularly valuable for assessing children who may prematurely wake from sleep or sedation. Advanced, next-generation ASSR equipment enables individual frequency stimulus intensity adjustments while other frequencies are still being tested. [10] In traditional first-generation ASSR, stimuli at the same intensity are given for multiple frequencies to both ears, and intensity changes are made only after completing tests for all frequencies. With newer systems, individual intensity adjustments are possible before completing testing for all frequencies, resulting in a shorter overall testing duration. Multiple studies have indicated no significant differences in hearing thresholds between single-frequency and multifrequency or monaural and binaural testing. [2] [10] [26] The average testing time for ASSR is 20 to 25 min to obtain 8 thresholds, significantly shorter than the ABR test duration of 32 to 60 min. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Auditory Steady-State Response -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The auditory steady-state response (ASSR) is an objective electrophysiological test to estimate hearing thresholds. ASSR is a newer procedure than other auditory evoked potential (AEP) tests, such as auditory brainstem response (ABR); both ASSR and ABR provide similar hearing threshold results, but ASSR provides more consistent, statistically valid results in less time. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Withdrawal Response -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The reflex arc is the basic unit of a reflex, which involves neural pathways acting on an impulse before that impulse has reached the brain. Instead of directly traveling to the brain, sensory neurons of a reflex arc synapse in the spinal cord. This is an important evolutionary adaptation for survival, which allows faster actions by activating spinal motor neurons instead of delaying reaction time by signals first having to go to the brain.

retrieved_docs.title: Auditory Steady-State Response -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The outcomes of ASSR testing in children are influenced by various factors, including the ambient noise levels, with higher noise associated with smaller amplitudes, recording time, with longer durations reducing residual noise in the electroencephalogram and aiding the recognition of near-threshold responses, and the signal-to-noise ratio, which compares response amplitude in ASSR to background EEG amplitude. Infants typically exhibit smaller ASSR response amplitudes than adults, necessitating noise reduction and stringent criteria for detection. The age of the infant also plays a role, as ASSR results are less reliable in younger infants and exhibit greater variability; ASSR is more effective when conducted on healthy, full-term infants older than 2 weeks of age. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Auditory Steady-State Response -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: ASSR testing procedures involve the presentation of auditory stimuli, typically in the form of tones or modulated sounds. The stimuli are relayed using either supra-aural headphones or insert headphones. Electrodes are placed on the scalp to record the electrical responses generated by the brain in response to these stimuli. Analysis of brain electrical activity permits audiologists and researchers to gather information about auditory thresholds, frequency-specific hearing abilities, and potential auditory processing disorders. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Multifocal Electroretinogram -- Technique or Treatment -- Implicit Time
retrieved_docs.content: Implicit time (time-to-peak) refers to the time needed for the electrical response to reach maximum amplitude. Implicit time is measured from stimulus onset to the corresponding wave-component peak and reflects the signal conduction rate.

retrieved_docs.title: Brainstem Auditory Evoked Response Test -- Issues of Concern -- Waveforms
retrieved_docs.content: Wave I: Wave I is the first positive deflection observed in the BAER waveform. It represents the initial activation of the auditory nerve fibers in the cochlea. Wave I is primarily generated by the synchronous firing of the distal portion of the auditory nerve fibers and is typically observed within 1 to 2 milliseconds after the auditory stimulus. However, it is important to note that the precise origin of Wave I is still debated among researchers. Factors such as stimulus intensity, electrode placement, and individual variations can influence the amplitude and latency of Wave I. Due to its variability, Wave I may be less reliable for clinical interpretation compared to other components. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2673
retrieved_docs.content: The basilar artery (arteria basilaris) is identified as a common site where transient cerebral ischemia can occur. Transient cerebral ischemia typically follows a transitory clinical course, indicating that its symptoms are brief and reversible. In Arabic, transient cerebral ischemia is referred to as “نوبة إقفارية عابرة,” underscoring its nature as a short‑lasting ischemic episode.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2126
retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia is characterized by a transitory clinical course, indicating that the neurological deficits associated with this brief interruption of cerebral blood flow are temporary and typically resolve quickly without permanent damage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia is described as having a transitory clinical course, indicating that its neurological deficits are brief and resolve quickly. This condition corresponds to the Arabic term نوبة إقفارية عابرة, which denotes the same short‑lasting, reversible episode of cerebral ischemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1831
retrieved_docs.content: Transitory denotes a clinical course that encompasses episodes of غشي and characterizes transient cerebral ischemia. Transient cerebral ischemia, which is also referred to by the Arabic term نوبة إقفارية عابرة, shares this transitory pattern, linking the concept of fleeting neurological impairment with the manifestation of غشي. Understanding this relationship highlights that both غشي and نوبة إقفارية عابرة represent brief, reversible events within a transitory clinical trajectory.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 938
retrieved_docs.content: The entity “has child” is linked to withdrawal and rebound effects, which can manifest as rebound tachycardia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 966
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of nervous system injury is classified as متلازمة ما بعد الارتجاج المخي (post‑concussion syndrome), representing a delayed neurological consequence that can develop after a brain concussion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 798
retrieved_docs.content: Asymptomatic occlusion of the basilar artery is evaluated within the framework of General wellbeing, which is interpreted by the Arabic term غشي, denoting a brief loss of consciousness or fainting. The notion of a Transitory clinical course is linked to غشي, indicating that short‑lasting episodes of altered consciousness may represent the manifestation of a transient process. This transitory course characterizes transient cerebral ischemia, a condition that is synonymous with the Arabic phrase نوبة إقفارية عابرة (transient ischemic attack). Consequently, an asymptomatic basilar artery occlusion may be considered in the context of overall wellbeing and, if symptoms arise, may present with brief episodes of غشي that reflect the transient nature of cerebral ischemic events such as a transient ischemic attack.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1890
retrieved_docs.content: Syncope is classified as a specific form of فقدان الوعي (loss of consciousness), indicating that syncope falls under the broader parent concept of loss of consciousness. This hierarchical relationship helps clinicians categorize episodes of transient loss of awareness and guides appropriate diagnostic and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1020
retrieved_docs.content: Asymptomatic occlusion of the basilar artery is evaluated in relation to a patient’s general wellbeing, a concept that is further interpreted by the term غشي. The term غشي, which denotes a transient clinical state, is also described as the clinical course of a transitory episode. Thus, the presence of an asymptomatic basilar artery occlusion may be understood through the lens of general wellbeing and may manifest as a brief, transient episode characterized by غشي.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2354
retrieved_docs.content: The arteria basilaris is identified as the anatomical location where transient cerebral ischemia occurs. Transient cerebral ischemia, also referred to by its Arabic term نوبة إقفارية عابرة, represents a brief episode of reduced cerebral blood flow that can produce temporary neurological deficits. Recognizing that transient cerebral ischemia originates in the basilar artery is important for clinicians, as ischemic events in this posterior circulation can lead to distinct clinical presentations and influence diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2668
retrieved_docs.content: embolie; arteria basilaris, referred to in Arabic as انصمام الشريان المخي, denotes an embolic occlusion of the basilar artery. This condition occurs when a thrombus or other embolic material blocks the basilar artery, which supplies blood to the brainstem, cerebellum, and posterior cerebral circulation. The resulting ischemia can lead to severe neurological deficits, including loss of consciousness, cranial nerve palsies, motor weakness, and potentially fatal outcomes if not promptly recognized and treated. Early diagnosis through neuroimaging and rapid reperfusion strategies are critical for improving patient prognosis and minimizing long‑term disability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 831
retrieved_docs.content: central role of peripheral nerves in the pathophysiology of diverse neurologic disorders and underscore the need for precise classification, thorough neurologic evaluation, and targeted management to guide diagnosis, treatment, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Transitory is described as a temporary medical condition whose clinical course is characterized by غشي, indicating that episodes of drowsiness or altered consciousness are a typical feature of its progression. This relationship underscores that patients experiencing a transitory condition may present with غشي as part of the condition’s natural course.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral infarction (“الاحتشاء”) results from occlusion, stenosis, embolism or thrombosis of the precerebral arteries, including embolic or thrombotic precerebral artery infarcts and basilar‑artery occlusion, and is pathophysiologically linked to “انسداد الأوعية الدموية” (vascular occlusion). Asymptomatic basilar‑artery occlusion, although sometimes perceived as a state of general wellbeing, may present with transient faintness (“غشي”). Within the hierarchy of nervous‑system circulatory disorders, “Circulatory Diseases and Syndromes of Nervous System” contains the child “حادثة وعائية دماغية” (cerebrovascular accident) and the parent “transient cerebral ischemia” (TIA, “نوبة إقفارية عابرة”), the latter being a brief, reversible reduction in cerebral blood flow. Cerebral artery sclerosis (“تصلب الشرايين المخية”) and intracranial arterial sclerosis (“تصلب عصيدي في المخ”) contribute to chronic arterial stiffening, increase the risk of cerebrovascular accidents, and are associated with

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: developmental disorders, and traumatic brain injury constitute an interconnected spectrum of nervous‑system disorders that inform comprehensive diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in neurology and neurosurgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 689
retrieved_docs.content: are contraindicated in patients with atrial fibrillation, underscoring the need for careful medication selection in this arrhythmia. Overall, the interconnections among transient cerebral ischemia, its transitory clinical course, cardiac electrophysiology studies, specific arrhythmic foci (atrial fibrillation and ventricular acceleration), associated diseases (rheumatic heart disease, transient ischemic attack), and medication contraindications highlight a comprehensive framework for understanding the diagnosis, evaluation, and management of related cardiovascular and cerebrovascular conditions.
